Can't Use TypeScript? Don't Worry — You Can Still Annotate Types With JSDoc

If your company's project can't use TypeScript but you still want TypeScript-style type checking, give JSDoc a shot. With a few annotations and a tiny config file, you get type hints, autocomplete, and even real type validation right inside VSCode — no TypeScript required.
What's an Algorithm, and Why Should You Know a Little About Them?
A casual intro post on what algorithms are and why you should know a bit about them. It's meant as a starting point, so I won't get into anything too complex.
A JavaScript Puzzle: Compute a Math Expression Without Using eval

I came across a fun little JavaScript puzzle the other day, so I jotted down my thought process as a quick post — maybe one day I'll come back and read it myself. The challenge: take a math expression stored as a string and compute its result, but you're not allowed to use eval. We'll look at why eval is a bad idea, and a couple of safer ways to solve it.
querySelector vs getElementById: What's the Difference, and the Quirks Nobody Warns You About

querySelector and getElementById are two of the DOM-selecting methods you'll reach for the most in real projects. But what's actually different between them? This post walks through how each one works, the little quirks they hide (case sensitivity, returning null, where you can call them), the performance trade-off, and a handy jQuery-style shortcut.
Why You Shouldn't Call hasOwnProperty Directly (and What ESLint Wants Instead)

ESLint has a rule that yells at you for calling hasOwnProperty directly on an object. At first it feels overly strict, but there's a real reason behind it. This post walks through why obj.hasOwnProperty('key') can break, how an object property can shadow the prototype method, and why Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call is the safer way to go.
You Don't Have to Read Clean Code to Write Decent Code

This post took me close to a year to write — it's a collection of real code smells I've seen in my time as a TA, anonymized and cleaned up. Not a Clean Code book recap; more like "here are common patterns that hurt readability, and how to write them so future-you (or your reviewer) doesn't suffer." Naming, var vs let/const, ES6 destructuring, comments, formatting, ternaries, loops, and not mixing Composition API with Options API.
JavaScript Core (82) — Async/Await: Writing Reusable Error Catching

Wrapping every async/await call in its own try...catch gets old fast. In this post we walk through pulling the catch logic out into a single reusable function — and then fixing the version that breaks the moment you need to pass arguments in, using a bit of closure magic.
JavaScript Core Concepts (81) - ES6: Async/Await - Rewriting a Real Example with Async Function

We've covered a lot of theory so far, but honestly it doesn't click until you apply it to something real. So in this post we'll look at how Async Function actually gets used in practice — rewriting a real Axios example to see how much cleaner the code gets.
JavaScript Core (80) - ES6 Async/Await: How Async Functions and Promises Are Actually Connected

Let's talk about how Async Functions and Promises are connected, and why I keep saying you should get comfortable with Promise before touching Async and Await. Turns out an async function quietly turns into a Promise when you run it, which is exactly why the two play so nicely together.
JavaScript Core (79) - ES6 Async/Await: Why Bother, and the Basics

Time for a new chapter — Async and Await. In this post we'll figure out why you'd want to use Async/Await in the first place, and walk through how it actually works, from rewriting a Promise to handling errors with try/catch.
